title: Capcut Crash Course - Layers & Masking
note: CapCut course/foundation lesson — educational, not a single recreatable effect. Kept as a technique reference.
narration_transcript: We're gonna talk about layers or I like to refer as building your video cake. If you've ever built a cake layer by layer then you're already gonna be familiar with the concept we're about to dive into, working with multiple layers. Think of layers as the tears of the cake. Just like you start building a cake from the bottom up and your editor we're gonna begin with our base layer or our primary footage and then stack additional layers on top. These can be text, images overlays, or effects by stacking these layers you have the flexibility to blend elements, adjust their timing, and create mind blowing visual effects. It's like adding layers and toppings to your cake until you're happy with it. This also brings up the point typically, less is more. Just like a cake your video is built layer by layer each adding its own flavor and texture. For now keep that kick analogy in your mind and get ready cause I'm gonna show you a demo of a practical example to help you understand what I'm talking about. Catch you in the next video. When creating visual effects for your videos like this there's three main concepts that you really need to get a good grasp on. That's masking, keyframes and layers. I'm gonna try something a little bit different and give you a visual demo of how these layers work together and then how keyframes and masking comes into play. So when we're thinking of layers, there's always a base layer, right. We have layer 1, this would be our main footage usually this layer would be the layer that is less manipulated. No masking, no keyframes, it's very rare that we're gonna add any of that stuff so when you think of it, we have layer 2, here and the problem with layering them like how we talked about layering your cake is you stack your layers, right. The problem is that when you start stacking layers if they're the same size you can no longer see the bottom layer. This is where masking comes in handy. If we wanted to combine these two shots then we could use masking as a technique to reveal what we want or do not want to see. For instance if we added a split mask right down the middle. We added this split mask we would be left with this, second layer on top revealing only what's left over of the bottom layer okay so it works the same way with the different mask if we needed it to be smaller and inside and we made it a rectangle mask it would basically create this so there's our rectangle mask and we're still seeing layer 1 behind it but only where the mask is not active. Let's think about an actual example here, right. If we have our base layer this could be a scene with clouds in the sky and say we wanna bring in some mountains right so this could be our layer two and if we use a mask or a green screen yeah we can start building our scene by stacking layers on top of each other. We have our our main plate then we have layer 2 which is these mountains, so this is just two layers it's pretty straightforward when you start introducing more than two layers that's when it can get really tricky and confusing. If we wanted to bring in another layer and say we wanted, say this this was a piece of footage of a person on a green screen. This is technically our layer 3, right. We have our person who was shot and just imagine this as a green screen so we would either key out the background which would be green or we use the cut out module to remove the background from our subject. 3 so we have layer No. This is how masking is used to blend layers together. Keyframes are the animation that makes it happen so if you picture our scene here, if we wanted this object to animate in, what we would do is we would go to a point in our timeline and we would set a keyframe with our person over here. We would go forward in time and if we move our person then it sets that keyframe later in the timeline automatically, so now we're dealing with two keyframes and what happens is now when you click play it's gonna go back to the earlier part of the timeline and what's gonna happen is as it's playing through the timeline it's this person is gonna move from one keyframe to the other because we locked their position at point A and point B. That's a very basic demonstration of how the layers and masking work together. Hopefully that helps but as always let us know if you have any questions and I'll see you in the next video. Hello, creative minds. By the end of this module you'll have a solid understanding of what masking is and how to use it to add a creative touch to your videos. Let's dive in. First off let's demystify what masking is. In the realm of video editing, masking allows you to isolate and manipulate specific areas of your video. Masking is like the artist brush and video editing. It enables you to blend reveal, hide and create intriguing visuals. Sometimes masking is used practically to fix things, sometimes it's used as more of a creative tool. Imagine you wanna reveal text in your video but not the usual pop up way you can have text appear behind an object using masking to integrate that text in the scene and make it much more engaging. By isolating specific areas you can blend different video clips seamlessly, adding a cinematic touch to your content. Let's say you have a clip and you wanna adjust the color on only one element in the frame, masking allows you to isolate that element and apply color correction making it stand out or blend differently with the environment. May seem a bit complex at first but with practice you'll be creating stunning and visually rich content in no time. I'll show you some examples in the next video. Let's take a look at a couple of simple layers here. We have this black layer and a white layer. To demonstrate masking I'm gonna take my white layer overlay it to drop it down and we'll put it right on top of the black layer. Now all we're seeing is white, okay. If we go to the mask tool and we select Split Mask, it's gonna split that layer right in half, so now we're seeing the white layer on the top. The top half of the screen and the black layer on the bottom half. If we move that back and forth kind of see what's happening there it's almost like a doorway or a window so wherever we don't have the mask we're seeing through to the black layer which is actually behind the white layer. So with our masks selected we can move that around, we can use our fingers to rotate the mask so if you want it to be diagonal or we can change it so now if we change it to a circle mask. It's creating a little bit of a window there so wherever we move that circle is where we're gonna see the top layer. If we wanted to swap it and actually use that as a window to see through to the bottom layer we would click invert down here in the bottom left corner and it would reverse the position of the mask. If we wanted it to smoothly transition from one side to the other we would use the feather here and now we have a smoother outline on that mask. We can change it to a rectangle and if we click invert again now we're seeing that white layer on top. Just like if we had a cake and we were looking down on it, the top layer is what we would see first, right? If we cut it out that's like creating a mask where this comes in handy and the mask that I use the most is the split mask and it would be if I have two clips that are trying to blend together and I want one to show on the left and one to show on the right so basically we would take this split mask and we would rotate it. Usually would have some type of feather to smooth that so that you wouldn't see the harsh edge in the middle of the two clips and it would blend a lot smoother. So that's the basics of what a mask is used for.
